I think it's mainly a layperson's view of what a data race is, but it's not accurate. Okay, so I'm going to talk to you about the workflow for dynamic data race detection. The idea is, in the context of thread sanitizers specifically, you compile the program with a special instrumentation flag.

So when your compiler is compiling all the functions of a given source file, it instruments... huge number of operations with calls into a race detection library. Every thread launch is compiled specially. Every thread join is compiled specially. Mutex acquires and releases are treated specially. And quite critically, every read from a location that might be shared is prepended with a call into an instrumentation library. And every write to a location that might be shared is prepended with a call into an instrumentation library.

Furthermore, atomic operations are also treated specially. The idea is that if the race detection algorithm is going to be able to detect races between writes and writes, or writes and reads, it's going to have to record metadata about writes and reads. And it can't know in advance which ones to focus on, because it doesn't know what your program's going to do. The compiler's got very limited insight into the dynamic behavior of your program. So to be able to make sure all possible races have a chance of being detected, the compiler needs to instrument every single read and write unless it can prove that the read or write will not be to shared memory.

How can it prove that something's not to shared memory? Well, not very often. So if you had a function that declared a local variable, then if you don't leak the address of that variable, then that write would be, writes that variable would be guaranteed to not be shared. So if we've got a function f, B equals the address of A.

We can see by trivial alias analysis that p is going to be addressed in A, and then we can see that writing to p is not going to affect anything shared with another thread. There's no way that another thread could be accessing this memory referred to by p, unless it does some awful pointer arithmetic, like just using some raw pointer value and by chance hacking into the stack of this thread, which is not in the scope of this analysis.

Whereas if, for example, we would say something like, now, let's say we do something like call G with the address of P. And now I do star P becomes 42. So this local variable P, which is a pointer, Now we've leaked its address. We've called another function g, writing the address of the pointer.

And it's possible that that function g could cause p to point somewhere completely different. So unless the compiler has got access to the code of g and can do an analysis to prove that g doesn't do anything like that, the compiler must assume the worst and assume this is a possibly shared access. So remember, compilers are efficient tools that only do very limited work. analysis on your program looking for quick opportunities for optimization. Generally, they have to assume that your program might do anything they can't prove it doesn't do. Okay, so anything the compiler can't prove to be non-shared has got to be instrumented in case it accesses shared memory, in which case there's going to be a call into a dynamic database detection library.

What do these library functions do? Well, they update dynamic race detection states and the vector clock algorithm I'm gonna show you is the basis for the way the dynamic data race detection state operates and how it's updated. And the key thing is that this dynamic data race detection state is what allows the conditions for a data race to be checked as the program runs. These library functions have got to be mutually exclusive, otherwise we'd get data races in the data race detector.

So we've got a bunch of state, which is the data race analysis state, And every read or write to a possibly shared location is causing that state to get updated. And obviously in a concurrent program, a bunch of those accesses will genuinely be from multiple threads that are executing concurrently. So the data race detection state itself has got to be protected behind the scenes by synchronization. And I'm not going to go into details of how that works, but it's going to involve read-modify-write operations in very carefully engineered use of Atomics.

So the race detection algorithm is optimized in many clever ways, but nevertheless, it's going to be a lot slower than running natively. This is a non-negative integer, so a thread's logical clock starts at 1, and it's going to tick up occasionally when the thread performs certain actions.

In particular, it's going to tick up every time the thread releases a mutex. So if I'm a thread, my logical clock starts at 1, it stays at 1 until I release a mutex, then it becomes 2. things that do until I release another mutex, and then it becomes three. Of course, to release those mutexes, I need to acquire them, but that doesn't affect my logical clock. My logical clock starts at one and goes up every time I release a mutex. Okay, the problem with the vector clock algorithm is really space inefficient.

It requires a vector clock for every possible shared memory location, And remember, as I sketched to you earlier on the board, the compiler is going to be very conservative in figuring out which locations are possibly shared. It will only be able to deem a location as not possibly shared if it can prove it will definitely not be shared between threads. In the fast track paper I mentioned earlier, there are some really nice optimizations. So one optimization is to only track the latest writes to a location.

The idea here is that instead of every location storing a full vector clock, instead just store information about the last thread to write to that location. And it turns out that that is enough to detect races on that location. And also, it's fine to only detect the latest read from a location until it's detected that multiple threads have read from a location. If only one thread is read from a location, and we only track the latest read from that thread, then any other thread that writes to that location in an unsynchronized way will detect a race.

The trouble is if we've got multiple threads reading from a location, because reads don't always participate in data races, and reads don't read over each other in the way that writes write over each other, we can miss data races there. So the idea of the fast track algorithm for writes is to, instead of storing the full clock, just store information about the last thread to have written to a location. And then for reads, initially just store information about the last thread to have read from a location until you observe a second thread reading from the location and then start tracking the full vector clock, which is kind of fiddly.

Another situation is that you can have a case where the same thread is reading from a location exclusively very often. And in this case, you can have a lot of possibly shared memory that's not actually shared, right? So you often get cases where you've got memory that looks like it might be shared, but in reality, it's not shared. It's just that the compiler can't prove this is shared. So only one thread will ever read from it, in which case this optimization of initially just storing a single piece of data about the latest thread to a regretful location works fine because no other thread ever will reach from that location.

However, being able to dynamically switch between tracking a full vector clock or just tracking a single reach from a location is problematic in really efficient implementations. And as a result, the thread sanitizer approach takes a more practical way forward, which is just track a small number of reads and accept that some data races will be lost in the mix.
